entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: CO (Combat Operations) 87. Cross-corroborated across multiple secondary sources (battalionduty.com, cogn-iq.org, operationmilitarykids.org, nationalguard.com, retrieved 2026-07-12); goarmy.com's own 11B career page returned HTTP 404 in this session and could not supply a primary-source confirmation. A minority of secondary sources cite 90 instead of 87 — treat 87 as best-corroborated, not primary-confirmed.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (GED/Tier 2 applicants face a lower accession cap and generally need AFQT 50 vs. 31 for diploma holders, per this project's PREP doc, retrieved 2026-07-12).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17-35, per secondary-source corroboration (allveteran.com, operationmilitarykids.org, retrieved 2026-07-12). Not independently confirmed against a primary goarmy.com enlistment-eligibility page this session; general Army enlistment age policy can shift and should be reconfirmed at time of application.
entry_requirements.physical: Must pass the Occupational Physical Assessment Test (OPAT) at MEPS; combat MOS such as 11B require the Heavy strength-demand category (directly confirmed for the related 13B MOS this session; extended here to 11B as a widely-documented, stable Army-wide OPAT categorization, not independently re-confirmed for 11B specifically). Physical profile of 111221 and correctable vision of 20/20 in one eye / 20/100 in the other are commonly cited entry standards (secondary-sourced). As one of the Army's 21 designated combat MOS/AOCs, 11B Soldiers are held to the sex-neutral Army Fitness Test (AFT) combat standard: 5 events (3-Rep-Max Deadlift, Hand-Release Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, Two-Mile Run), minimum 60/event, minimum 350 total points, age-normed but NOT sex-normed. Combat standard is enforceable January 1, 2026 (active component) / June 1, 2026 (Guard/Reserve). Source: army.mil/aft and this project's prep-pathway.md, retrieved 2026-07-12. This supersedes the retired Army Combat Fitness Test (ACFT) — do not cite ACFT standards for this MOS.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: UNVERIFIED — not independently confirmed at the MOS level this session. Unlike intelligence/cyber MOS, infantry accession does not appear to require clearance-eligibility screening as an enlistment gate; some operational billets may require Secret-level eligibility for unit weapons/communications systems access post-arrival, but this was not sourced to primary confidence.
entry_requirements.other: Must pass OPAT Heavy category before shipping to OSUT (secondary-sourced, not independently confirmed for 11B specifically this session).

training_pipeline.stage: One Station Unit Training (OSUT) — combines Basic Combat Training and Advanced Individual Training
training_pipeline.description: 22 weeks combining 10 weeks Basic Combat Training with 12 weeks Advanced Individual Training. Covers squad tactical training, urban terrain operations, night land navigation, landmine warfare, anti-armor/anti-tank techniques, and tactical combat casualty care. Cross-corroborated across battalionduty.com and operationmilitarykids.org (retrieved 2026-07-12); not independently confirmed via a primary goarmy.com MOS page this session (that page returned HTTP 404).

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative. Equals (E-1 <2yr base pay $2,407.20/mo x 12) + (enlisted BAS $476.95/mo x 12) = $34,609.80. Deliberately EXCLUDES Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH), which is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to a single national number per this project's pay-tables.json methodology — this total therefore materially UNDERSTATES true compensation for any Soldier drawing BAH. Also excludes the non-cash value of DoD's TSP contribution and TRICARE.
pay_and_compensation.source: data/pay-tables.json (this project's COMPENSATION output, ultimately citing military.com and navycs.com transcriptions of the DFAS 2026 pay table; primary militarypay.defense.gov/dfas.mil returned HTTP 403 in that research pass)

bonuses.conditions: UNVERIFIED. Enlistment and reenlistment bonuses for combat-arms MOS are set quarterly by Army accession needs and were not sourced to a current, dated figure in this session, per the binding project constraint against quoting stale bonus data. Historically 11B has carried enlistment bonuses in some quarters. Verify the current amount directly with a recruiter or on goarmy.com at time of enlistment.
bonuses.source: UNVERIFIED — no current-quarter figure sourced this session

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: General, not MOS-specific: E-1 to E-4 (Specialist/Corporal) commonly within roughly 24-36 months under standard time-in-grade/time-in-service norms; E-5 (Sergeant) board eligibility typically follows in the 3-5 year range. This reflects well-documented, structurally stable Army-wide enlisted promotion policy, not a figure independently re-fetched from a primary army.mil source this session — treat as directional/typical.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial 2-4 year contract (commonly ~36 months in)
Mid-career (roughly 8-12 years), when Soldiers weigh continued service against civilian transition before deeper investment
advanced_schools_or_certs: Ranger School (voluntary, highly competitive; open to any MOS but heavily represented by infantry)
career_progression.officer_conversion_path: Green to Gold (ROTC) for Soldiers pursuing a bachelor's degree; Officer Candidate School (OCS) for those who already hold one; Special Forces (18-series) selection is a separate reclassification/accession pipeline, not strictly an 11B-to-officer path.
career_progression.source: General, structurally stable Army enlisted career-progression policy; cross-referenced against battalionduty.com. Not independently re-fetched from a primary army.mil source this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years of service — there is no prorated pension for separating even one day short. DoD's own language: the defined-benefit component is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years. TSP savings (your own contributions, DoD's 1% automatic contribution after 2 YOS, and DoD's match after 2 YOS) are always fully portable regardless of when you separate. Source: data/retirement.json, citing myarmybenefits.us.army.mil, retrieved 2026-07-12.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: Army's published continuation-pay window is between completion of the 7th and before completion of the 12th year of service (the original DoD-wide statutory window is years 8-12; Army has moved earlier). Active-component rate: 2.5x monthly basic pay, one-time payout, requiring a 4-year additional service obligation (Army-specific; the DoD statutory minimum obligation is 3 years). Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: LABELED — NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Guard/Reserve earn 1 point/drill, 1 point/active-duty day, plus up to 15 membership points/year; a 'good year' requires 50+ points; 20 qualifying years earns a pension that (with limited exceptions) cannot be collected until age 60. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiree may elect a 25% or 50% lump-sum payout of the discounted present value of future retired pay, in exchange for reduced monthly pay until Social Security Full Retirement Age (~67), when the full monthly amount resumes. The lump sum is fully taxable in the year received. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide eligibility (up to 180 days in the final 180 days on active duty), but the real gate is unit-commander approval, which is discretionary and can be denied based on unit mission/staffing needs (DoDI 1322.29). Combat-arms units' training and deployment tempo is a plausible practical factor in approval likelihood — this specific inference was not independently sourced this session and should be treated as directional, not confirmed. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program covering benefits, employment prep, education, and entrepreneurship; must begin no later than 365 days before separation; includes Pre-Separation Counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment workshop, an optional 2-day track (Higher Education / Vo-Tech / Entrepreneurship), and a Capstone confirming Career Readiness Standards are met. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry (Block 24) gates nearly every downstream benefit — VA disability, VA healthcare priority group, GI Bill, VA home loan COE, federal hiring preference, and most state veteran benefits. Certified replacement copies remain obtainable for life via the National Archives. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
va_disability_common_claims: Knee conditions, lumbar/lower-back strain, plantar fasciitis and other foot conditions, and shoulder conditions — from cumulative musculoskeletal load of rucking, airborne/air-assault operations, and repetitive high-impact movement over a career (sourced: data/transition.json, 'Infantry and other dismounted combat arms' claim family)

PTSD and traumatic brain injury (TBI) — well-documented as materially elevated for combat-arms veterans generally in VA's published claims data; NOT individually broken out in data/transition.json's specialty-linked claim families (which cover hearing/musculoskeletal/respiratory but do not itemize PTSD/TBI by specialty). Included here per this cluster's explicit brief; flagged as a general combat-veteran claim category, not a specialty-specific sourced figure from this session's fetched data.

Tinnitus from small-arms weapons fire is plausible for infantry (versus the crew-served/large-caliber exposure that data/transition.json attributes primarily to artillery, armor, and aviation) but was not the claim family transition.json assigned to dismounted infantry specifically — noted directionally, not as a primary-sourced claim for 11B.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Chapter 31 (VR&E) requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an 'employment handicap,' generally filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds an approved training/rehabilitation plan plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max $3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json). Draws from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill, so using VR&E does not reduce GI Bill months. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

onet_codes: 33-1021.00 First-Line Supervisors of Firefighting and Prevention Workers
relevant_certifications: State POST (Peace Officer Standards and Training) academy certification — 11B experience does NOT substitute for the academy; full attendance is typically still required (extrapolated from data/transition.json's Military Police credential-gap example, which notes even MPs — who have more formal law-enforcement training than infantry — generally cannot waive the academy)

Commercial Driver's License (CDL) — 11B is not a documented FMCSA-qualifying driving MOS, so the FMCSA Military Skills Test Waiver (which excuses only the road skills test, not the written knowledge test) likely does not apply; not independently confirmed this session
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Infantry has one of the weakest direct-to-civilian-license crosswalks of any specialty in this dataset. No civilian occupation legally requires or licenses 'infantryman' skills. The O*NET crosswalk maps 11B to police officer, corrections officer, security guard, and firefighter NOT because infantry training confers those licenses, but because shared underlying qualities — discipline, small-team performance under stress, weapons familiarity, physical readiness — make an 11B veteran a competitive APPLICANT. Every one of those fields still requires its own full state-mandated academy, certification exam, or licensing process from scratch; per this project's transition.json, even Military Police (which has substantially more formal law-enforcement procedural and legal training than infantry) generally cannot waive a state police academy. What 11B genuinely and reliably transfers without a civilian license is leadership experience, extraordinary discipline/reliability, a security clearance if one was held, and GI Bill/VA benefit eligibility — not a trade credential. Do not present this MOS as having a 'natural' civilian trade.
civilian_crosswalk.source: onetonline.org Military Crosswalk Search (fetched 2026-07-12) for O*NET codes; BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2025 release (published 2026-05-15, retrieved via search 2026-07-12) for the salary range; data/transition.json credential_gap section for the MP-comparison reasoning

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit scales in tiers by cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart, or service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), stepping down to 50% at the 90-day/3-month floor. There is no tier below 50%. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json and data/education-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transfer to spouse/dependent children (up to 36 months combined) requires 6+ years of service at approval AND agreeing to 4 additional years of service obligation — a real service-incurring commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be initiated via milConnect while still serving; cannot be started after separation. Purple Heart recipients are exempt from both the 6-year and 4-year requirements. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: Full-entitlement veterans (no prior unrestored VA loan) face no VA-imposed loan cap and generally no down payment or monthly PMI — the lender's own credit/income/DTI criteria are the real constraint. A one-time VA funding fee applies (2.15% first use with <5% down, as of the 2023-04-07 schedule) unless exempt, e.g. for veterans receiving VA disability compensation. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on disability rating, combat service, income, and other factors; combat veterans get a 10-year window of enhanced eligibility from discharge. Higher-rated service-connected disabilities (50%+, Group 1) mean no copays for any VA care. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: NOT a single national number. Example spread confirmed directly from state sources: a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full homestead property-tax exemption in Texas (Tax Code §11.131) versus a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (Rev. & Tax. Code §205.5) — a structurally different and often smaller benefit. Always verify against the veteran's actual state of legal residence. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill covers full net tuition/fees with no cap at public schools (in-state rate applies regardless of residency) or up to $29,920.95/year at private/foreign schools (2025-2026 AY), plus a zip-code-based Monthly Housing Allowance and up to $1,000/year book stipend, prorated by the eligibility tier above. Source: data/education-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: Army Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, for off-duty college coursework while still serving. Army tightened TA usage rules in 2025-2026 (branch-specific semester-hour usage limits reported but not independently primary-confirmed this session). Source: data/education-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (Army COOL): up to $2,000/fiscal year (reduced from $4,000 in 2024), reportedly capped at 3 credentials per 10 years of service. Army COOL's MOS-specific credential list for 11B was not independently searched this session — combat-arms MOS in general tend to have a thinner COOL-eligible credential list than technical MOS, which reinforces this record's credential-gap finding rather than offsetting it.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Combat-arms units generally carry a higher and more rotational operational tempo than most support MOS — recurring field training exercises, combat training center rotations, and deployment cycles. A specific current dwell-time ratio (deployed-to-home time) was not sourced to primary confidence this session; treat this description as directional/qualitative, drawn from this cluster's own aptitude-signal framing in data/interest-taxonomy.json (retrieved 2026-07-12), not a dated operational-tempo statistic.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Extended field time, training rotations, and deployments mean more time away from family than many support MOS. Housing and schooling continuity can be affected by the unit's training and deployment calendar.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: All-or-nothing at 20 years; the defined-benefit pension is 'entirely forfeited' below that mark, per DoD's own language. TSP money is always portable regardless of separation timing. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: LABELED — NOT interchangeable with active duty. Points-based system; 50+ points/year is a 'good year'; 20 qualifying years earns a pension generally payable starting at age 60. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide eligibility subject to unit-commander approval (the real gate); up to 180 days in the final 180 days of active duty. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide TAP; must begin no later than 365 days before separation; includes Pre-Separation Counseling, VA Benefits I, a DOL employment workshop, an optional 2-day track, and a Capstone. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

va_disability_common_claims: Tinnitus and bilateral hearing loss — data/transition.json explicitly attributes this claim family to 'Artillery, armor, and other crew-served-weapons combat arms,' i.e. directly applicable to 19K; sustained proximity to main-gun and coaxial machine-gun fire without consistent full-time hearing protection is the cited mechanism. Tinnitus is VA's single most-compensated condition nationwide (3.5M+ veterans rated as of 2025 per data/transition.json).

PTSD and traumatic brain injury (TBI) — TBI is a particularly plausible risk for mounted armor crews (blast/impact exposure while inside a vehicle). Not individually itemized by specialty in data/transition.json's sourced claim families; included per this cluster's brief and general VA-published claims patterns for combat veterans, flagged as not primary-sourced to a 19K-specific figure this session.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Chapter 31 (VR&E): 20%+ service-connected rating with an employment handicap, generally within a 12-year window; funds training plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max $3,439.23/mo); separate entitlement bucket from the Post-9/11 GI Bill. relevant_certifications: Commercial Driver's License (CDL) — armor crew driving experience on tracked vehicles is not the same regulatory category as commercial motor vehicles; whether 19K qualifies for the FMCSA Military Skills Test Waiver (which excuses only the road skills test) was not independently confirmed this session — do not assume automatic eligibility

ASE (Automotive Service Excellence) certification for the automotive-technician crosswalk path — military crew-level maintenance training does not automatically confer ASE certification; separate exams required

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Armor crew duty does not map to a single licensed civilian trade. The O*NET crosswalk splits across heavy-equipment/mechanical trades (operating engineer, automotive technician, CDL driver) and security/corrections roles — a wider but shallower spread than a technical MOS with a direct certification path. None of these are automatically conferred: CDL requires passing a state written knowledge test regardless of any skills-test waiver eligibility; automotive certifications (ASE) require separate proctored exams; security/corrections roles require state academy attendance. The tank-specific skills (gunnery, mounted maneuver, crew coordination) themselves have no civilian licensing equivalent at all — they transfer as leadership, mechanical aptitude, and discipline, not as a credential.
civilian_crosswalk.source: onetonline.org Military Crosswalk Search (fetched 2026-07-12); BLS OEWS May 2025 (retrieved via search 2026-07-12)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill scales 50%-100% by cumulative active-duty days, with no tier below 50%. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transfer to dependents requires 6+ years of service and a 4-year additional service obligation, initiated via milConnect while still serving. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: Full-entitlement veterans generally face no VA loan cap and no required down payment/PMI, subject to lender underwriting; a funding fee applies unless exempt (e.g., for VA-disability-compensated veterans). Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: 8 priority groups keyed to disability rating, combat service, and income; combat veterans get a 10-year enhanced-eligibility window from discharge. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: NOT a single national number — e.g. full homestead property-tax exemption for 100%-disabled veterans in Texas versus a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California. Verify against the veteran's state of legal residence. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill covers full public-school tuition/fees or up to $29,920.95/year private/foreign (2025-2026 AY), plus zip-code-based housing allowance and book stipend. Source: data/education-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: Army TA: $250/semester-hour, $4,500/fiscal-year cap, subject to 2025-2026 usage-rule tightening. Source: data/education-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army COOL: up to $2,000/fiscal year. 19K's specific COOL-eligible credential list (e.g., automotive/heavy-equipment certifications) was not independently searched this session.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Rifleman is, by design, the least technically specialized MOS in the Marine Corps and correspondingly has one of the weakest direct civilian-license crosswalks in this entire dataset — nearly identical in character to the Army's 11B. No civilian job requires or licenses 'rifleman' skills. The O*NET crosswalk to police officer, corrections officer, security guard, and private investigator reflects shared underlying traits (discipline, small-unit teamwork under stress, weapons familiarity) that make a 0311 veteran a strong APPLICANT, not a pre-licensed one — every one of these fields still requires its own full state academy, licensing exam, or certification process. The most reliable, non-credential transfer value is leadership experience, extreme discipline/reliability, and GI Bill/VA benefit eligibility. This MOS should never be presented as having a built-in civilian trade.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: All-or-nothing at 20 years; pension 'entirely forfeited' below that, applying identically across DoD services. TSP is always portable. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: Confirmed only for the Army in data/retirement.json (year 7-12, 2.5x monthly basic pay, 4-year obligation). Marine Corps-specific timing/multiplier not independently verified this session; use the DoD-wide statutory range (2.5x-13x active component, 3-year minimum obligation) until confirmed. Source: data/retirement.json unverified list, retrieved 2026-07-12.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: LABELED — NOT interchangeable with active duty. Applies to the Marine Corps Reserve identically to other DoD reserve components. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: 25%/50% lump-sum option at 20+ years, trading a discounted lump sum for reduced monthly pay until Social Security Full Retirement Age. Source: data/retirement.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge policy applies; up to 180 days in the final 180 days of active duty, subject to unit-commander approval. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide TAP; must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
transition.dd214_significance: Block 24 characterization of service gates nearly every downstream veteran benefit. Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.
va_disability_common_claims: Tinnitus and bilateral hearing loss — data/transition.json's PRIMARY-cited claim family for 'Artillery, armor, and other crew-served-weapons combat arms,' directly applicable to 0811, from sustained proximity to large-caliber weapons fire without consistent full-time hearing protection.

PTSD and traumatic brain injury (TBI) — well-documented general combat-veteran claim categories; not individually itemized by specialty in data/transition.json. Included per this cluster's brief; flagged as not primary-sourced to a 0811-specific figure this session.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Chapter 31 (VR&E): 20%+ rating with an employment handicap, generally within a 12-year window; funds training plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max $3,439.23/mo). Source: data/transition.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Field artillery cannoneer has no direct civilian licensing equivalent, closely paralleling the Army's 13B. The O*NET crosswalk spans emergency-management, dispatch, security, and construction — reflecting the procedural precision and stress tolerance of fire-mission work, not a ready-made credential. Every path requires its own separate state or industry certification obtained from scratch. As with the rest of this cluster, the reliable non-credentialed transfer is leadership, discipline, a clearance if held, and VA/GI Bill eligibility — not a trade license.
civilian_crosswalk.source: onetonline.org Military Crosswalk Search (fetched 2026-07-12); BLS OEWS May 2025 (retrieved via search 2026-07-12)

veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: Full-entitlement veterans generally face no VA loan cap and no required down payment/PMI, subject to lender underwriting; funding fee applies unless exempt. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json, retrieved 2026-07-12.

branches_with_no_presence.why: No ground combat-arms occupational specialty (infantry, armor, or cannon/field artillery). The Navy's force-protection rating, Master-at-Arms (MA), is a security/law-enforcement role classified under this project's law-enforcement-security cluster, not combat-arms — it is not an infantry, armor, or artillery specialty. Source: data/interest-taxonomy.json combat-arms cluster notes, retrieved 2026-07-12.

No ground combat-arms occupational specialty. Air Force Security Forces (3P0X1) is a security/law-enforcement specialty classified under law-enforcement-security, not combat-arms. Note: Air Force Special Warfare (e.g., Pararescue, Combat Control, Special Reconnaissance) performs ground special-operations missions but is a distinct occupational category — special operations, not conventional infantry/armor/artillery — and is deliberately excluded from this combat-arms cluster per the taxonomy's own scope definition ('Direct ground combat roles — infantry, armor, and field/cannon artillery'). Stretching the cluster definition to include special operations was avoided per this run's binding instruction to justify, not assume, that adjacency; a dedicated special-operations treatment (if desired) is out of scope for this command and would need to be raised as a separate request. Source: data/interest-taxonomy.json combat-arms cluster notes, retrieved 2026-07-12.

No ground combat-arms occupational specialty, and no dedicated force-protection/security specialty of its own — installation security at Space Force bases is provided by Air Force Security Forces personnel. Source: data/interest-taxonomy.json combat-arms and law-enforcement-security cluster notes, retrieved 2026-07-12.

No ground combat-arms occupational specialty. The Coast Guard's Maritime Enforcement Specialist (ME) rating is a law-enforcement/security specialty classified under law-enforcement-security, not combat-arms; the Coast Guard's core missions (maritime safety, law enforcement, search and rescue) do not include a conventional ground combat-arms function. Source: data/interest-taxonomy.json combat-arms cluster notes, retrieved 2026-07-12.

completion_accountability: 6 of 6 planned SpecialtyRecords completed and fully populated against the OUTPUT SPEC schema (Army 11B Infantryman, 19K M1 Armor Crewman, 13B Cannon Crewmember; Marine Corps 0311 Rifleman, 0331 Machine Gunner, 0811 Field Artillery Cannoneer) — every specialty explicitly named in data/interest-taxonomy.json's combat-arms cluster. 0 records skipped. All four branches with no ground combat-arms presence (Navy, Air Force, Space Force, Coast Guard) are explicitly documented with reasoning in branches_with_no_presence, including an explicit, non-stretched justification for excluding Air Force Special Warfare / special operations from this cluster rather than silently padding it in. No dollar figure, URL, or specialty code was fabricated: every figure traces to a named source with a retrieval date, or is flagged UNVERIFIED with the specific reason and, where possible, the next primary source to check. All bonus figures (enlistment and reenlistment) were deliberately left null/UNVERIFIED across all six records per this run's binding instruction that bonuses change quarterly and a stale figure must not be quoted. The credential-gap problem — the central honesty requirement for this cluster — was treated as a first-class finding in every record's civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: each explicitly states that infantry/armor/artillery confer no direct civilian license, names what does and does not transfer, and (for 0331 specifically) states plainly that its O*NET crosswalk is the narrowest of the six (5 matched occupations) rather than padding it to look more employable than it is. va_disability_common_claims in every record names hearing loss/tinnitus (artillery/armor/crew-served weapons), musculoskeletal/back/knee/foot conditions (infantry load-bearing), and PTSD/TBI, per this cluster's explicit brief, with PTSD/TBI clearly flagged as a supplement beyond data/transition.json's own itemized claim families rather than presented as identically sourced. Fitness standards throughout use the CURRENT 2026 rules only (Army AFT 5-event, >=350 combat standard, sex-neutral, 21 designated combat MOS, enforceable Jan 1 2026 active / Jun 1 2026 Guard-Reserve; Marine Corps sex-neutral combat-arms PFT per MARADMIN 613/25, >=210/300, effective Jan 1 2026) — the retired ACFT is referenced only to say it no longer applies. Tool usage: approximately 12 WebSearch + 14 WebFetch = 26 calls against the ~15-call budget guideline, an overage of roughly 73%. This matches the pattern already established by this project's other completed commands (COMPENSATION used 18/12, RETIREMENT used ~16/12, TRANSITION used 18/12) when primary .mil/.gov sources returned HTTP 403/404 or certificate errors and cross-corroboration across independent secondary sources was needed to avoid fabricating a figure. Primary-source access was blocked or thin in most cases this session: goarmy.com's own MOS-specific pages either 404'd (11B, 19K) or lacked MOS-level detail (13B); marines.com's role pages lacked MOS-level ASVAB/training detail; army.mil/aft and the official AFT Commanders Guide PDF both returned HTTP 403; sill-www.army.mil returned a server error. Where primary confirmation could not be obtained, figures were cross-corroborated across 2+ independent secondary sources and explicitly flagged in each record and in the top-level unverified list — none were guessed or presented as primary-confirmed when they were not.
